title: Process Design, Process Types & Layout
simple_html:
Once you know what an operation must be good at (Lessons 2-3), you design how the work flows. Process design is about arranging tasks, people, and machines so that products or services move through the operation efficiently. Three linked ideas matter: process design, process types, and layout.

Process types: matching the process to the job

The right way to organise work depends on volume and variety. For manufacturing, the common types, from low volume/high variety to high volume/low variety, are:

  • Project - one big, unique job (building a bridge).
  • Jobbing - many small, custom jobs (a bespoke tailor).
  • Batch - groups of similar items made together (a bakery).
  • Mass/Line - high volume of standard items (a car assembly line).
  • Continuous - non-stop flow (an oil refinery).

Layout: arranging the physical space

Layout is how you physically position resources. The four basic layouts are:

  • Fixed-position - the product stays put and resources come to it (shipbuilding).
  • Process (functional) - similar machines grouped together (a hospital with departments).
  • Cell - resources for one product family grouped in one area.
  • Product (line) - resources arranged in the sequence of operations (assembly line).

Key takeaways

  • Low volume/high variety needs flexible processes and layouts; high volume/low variety needs efficient line processes.
  • Process type and layout should match each other, the product-process matrix shows the natural fit.
  • Getting this wrong, e.g. a job-shop layout for mass production, creates cost and chaos.
intermediate_html:
Process design turns the strategic priorities of Lesson 3 into a concrete arrangement of activities. It answers: what type of process should we run, and how should we lay out the resources physically? The driving variables are the same as the 4 Vs, above all volume and variety.

The five process types (manufacturing)

As volume rises and variety falls, the appropriate process type shifts:

TypeVolume / VarietyExample
ProjectVery low vol / very high varietyBuilding, film production
JobbingLow vol / high varietyBespoke furniture, tailoring
BatchMedium vol / medium varietyBakery, clothing manufacture
Mass / LineHigh vol / low varietyCar assembly, appliances
ContinuousVery high vol / very low varietyOil refining, steel, electricity

Service operations have an analogous spectrum: professional services (low volume, high contact, customised, e.g. consulting), service shops (medium, e.g. a bank branch), and mass services (high volume, low contact, standardised, e.g. an airport check-in).

The four layout types

Layout is the physical placement of transforming resources. The four basic types map roughly onto the process types:

  • Fixed-position layout: the transformed resource is too large or delicate to move, so resources come to it (shipbuilding, surgery). Suits projects.
  • Process (functional) layout: similar resources are grouped together; products travel between groups on varied routes (a machine shop, a hospital's departments). Suits jobbing/batch and high variety.
  • Cell layout: resources needed for a particular product family are grouped into a cell, combining some flow efficiency with variety. Suits batch with families.
  • Product (line) layout: resources are arranged in the fixed sequence the product needs; the product flows down the line (assembly line, cafeteria). Suits mass and continuous.

The trade-off is consistent: process layouts give flexibility at the cost of complex flow and high work-in-progress; product layouts give efficiency and low cost at the cost of flexibility and vulnerability to stoppages.

The product-process matrix

Hayes and Wheelwright's product-process matrix plots product structure (volume/variety) against process structure. Well-run operations lie on the diagonal, where the process type matches the product profile. Sitting off the diagonal is usually a mistake: top-right (a flexible jobbing process making a high-volume standard product) means needlessly high cost; bottom-left (a rigid line trying to make highly varied low-volume products) means lost flexibility and idle, inflexible assets. Firms occasionally choose an off-diagonal position deliberately for competitive reasons, but they pay for it.

Designing the line: balancing

For product layouts, line balancing assigns tasks to workstations so each takes roughly equal time, set by the cycle time the demand requires (the takt time). Poor balancing creates bottlenecks and idle stations.

Worked example

A furniture company makes (a) bespoke handmade cabinets and (b) a flat-pack bookshelf sold by the thousand. The cabinets are jobbing, best served by a process layout (skilled craftsmen and general tools grouped by function). The bookshelf is near-mass, best served by a product/line layout with balanced stations. Trying to make the bookshelf in the craft workshop would be hopelessly expensive per unit; trying to make bespoke cabinets on the bookshelf line would be impossible. The product-process matrix predicts exactly this: two different products require two different positions on the diagonal, which is why many firms physically separate, or "focus," their facilities (Skinner's focused factory).

How to apply it

(1) Profile each product/service by volume and variety; (2) select the matching process type; (3) choose the layout that supports that process type; (4) for line layouts, compute takt time and balance the line; (5) check the position on the product-process matrix and justify any off-diagonal choice. This sequence ensures the physical operation actually delivers the strategy's chosen objectives.
full_html:
Process design is the activity of configuring the transformation system, its process type, its layout, and the detailed flow of work, so that it delivers the performance objectives chosen in operations strategy (Lesson 3). It is where strategic intent becomes physical reality. The governing variables remain volume and variety (from the 4 Vs, Lesson 2), and the central organising idea is that the nature of the work should dictate the structure of the process. This lesson covers process types, layout types, the product-process matrix that links them, and the analytics of process design (cycle time, takt time, and line balancing).

Process types

Process types are categories of process design that fit particular volume-variety positions. In manufacturing, the canonical five (Slack et al.; Hayes and Wheelwright, 1979) run along the volume-variety diagonal:

  • Project processes - highly customised, low-volume, long-duration, discrete outputs (construction, large IT implementations). Resources are often dedicated to a single output.
  • Jobbing processes - low volume, high variety, with resources shared across many small, customised jobs (precision toolmaking, bespoke tailoring).
  • Batch processes - products made in groups; each batch follows a route, then the equipment is changed over for the next (bakeries, pharmaceuticals). The most common and flexible middle ground.
  • Mass (line) processes - high volume, low variety, narrow task variety, repetitive flow (automotive assembly, consumer electronics).
  • Continuous processes - very high volume, very low variety, materials flow without interruption, typically capital-intensive and process-technology-led (refining, steelmaking, electricity generation).

Schmenner (1986) and others provide the service analogue, segmenting by labour intensity and customisation/contact: professional services (high contact/customisation, low volume, e.g. management consultancy), service shops (intermediate, e.g. retail banks, hospitals), and mass services (low contact, high volume, standardised, e.g. utilities, airport security). Chase's (1978) customer-contact model underpins this, since visibility (a 4V) drives much of the service-process choice.

Layout types

Layout is the spatial arrangement of transforming resources and the consequent flow of transformed resources. There are four fundamental types:

LayoutLogicBest forTrade-off
Fixed-positionProduct is stationary; resources come to itProjects (ships, buildings, surgery)Maximum flexibility; very low resource utilisation, complex coordination
Process (functional)Like resources grouped; varied routingsJobbing/batch, high varietyFlexible; high WIP, long throughput times, complex flow
CellResources for a product family co-locatedBatch with identifiable familiesBalances flow and flexibility; requires good family grouping (group technology)
Product (line)Resources in operation sequence; product flowsMass/continuousLow unit cost, simple flow; rigid, vulnerable to stoppage/absence

The recurring trade-off is flexibility versus flow efficiency. Process layouts maximise flexibility but generate complex, criss-crossing flows, high work-in-progress inventory, and long throughput times; product layouts give smooth, fast, cheap flow but are rigid and exposed to disruption (one stalled station can halt the line). Cell layout, derived from group technology, is a deliberate hybrid that captures much of the flow benefit while preserving variety within a family, and it is closely associated with lean (Lesson 7).

The product-process matrix

Hayes and Wheelwright's (1979) product-process matrix is the integrating framework. It plots product structure (low-volume/high-variety to high-volume/standardised) against process structure (jumbled-flow job shop to continuous flow). Coherent operations lie on the diagonal, where process capability matches product requirements. Two off-diagonal regions carry penalties: the upper-right (an overly flexible process making standardised high-volume goods) incurs excessive cost and forgone scale economies; the lower-left (an overly rigid process forced to make varied low-volume goods) loses flexibility and saddles the firm with inappropriate, underused dedicated assets. The matrix also encodes a dynamic: as a product matures and volume grows, the firm should migrate down the diagonal (from jobbing to batch to line), and failure to do so is a classic strategic error. Deliberate off-diagonal positioning can be a source of advantage (e.g., mass customisation that pushes flexibility into a high-volume process), but it must be a conscious, resourced choice, not an accident.

Process analytics: throughput, cycle time, takt, and balancing

Process design is quantitative as well as structural. Key relationships:

  • Cycle time = the time between successive units leaving the process; it is set by the slowest stage (the bottleneck).
  • Takt time = available production time divided by customer demand; the rhythm the process must achieve to meet demand. Takt = available time / demand.
  • Throughput, WIP, and lead time are tied together by Little's Law (Lesson 5): WIP = throughput rate x flow time.
  • Line balancing allocates tasks to workstations so each station's work content is as close as possible to the required cycle time, minimising balancing loss (idle time). The theoretical minimum number of stations = total work content / cycle time, rounded up.

Worked calculation: a line must produce 480 units per 8-hour (480-minute) shift, so takt time = 480/480 = 1 minute per unit. If total work content is 4.5 minutes, the minimum number of stations = 4.5/1 = 4.5, rounded up to 5 stations. Balancing then distributes the 4.5 minutes of work across those 5 stations (0.9 min each ideally) to keep every station at or under the 1-minute cycle time; the balancing loss is (5 x 1 - 4.5)/(5 x 1) = 10%.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Confusing process type with layout. They are related but distinct, process type is the organising logic; layout is the physical arrangement. A batch process usually uses a process or cell layout, but the concepts are separable.
  • Drifting off the diagonal unintentionally. Operations often add variety over time without re-designing the process, ending up in the costly upper-right region.
  • Equating takt time with cycle time. Takt is demand-driven (what the market requires); cycle time is capability-driven (what the process delivers). The design goal is cycle time at or just below takt.
  • Ignoring the bottleneck. Throughput is governed by the slowest stage (Theory of Constraints, Goldratt); balancing and capacity decisions must target it.
  • Over-using product layout. Its low unit cost tempts managers, but its rigidity and stoppage-vulnerability make it wrong for volatile or high-variety demand.

Cross-links

Process design operationalises the strategic choices of Lesson 3 and is driven by the volume-variety logic of Lesson 2. Its analytics, takt, cycle time, bottlenecks, and Little's Law, lead directly into capacity, inventory, and planning-and-control (Lesson 5). Cell layout and line balancing are foundational to lean and JIT (Lesson 7), where flow and the elimination of waste are paramount, and the location of supply and make-or-buy decisions connect to supply chain management (Lesson 6). Process capability in the quality sense is developed in Lesson 8.
